quiz_questions.question_text: DNA is composed of two strands wound around each other. What is the name of this three-dimensional shape?

options.text: Beta-pleated sheet — a flat structure found in proteins
Double helix — two polynucleotide strands coiled around a common axis
Triple helix — three strands twisted together
Single helix — one coiled strand of nucleotides

quiz_questions.explanation: Watson and Crick discovered in 1953 that DNA has a double helix structure — two antiparallel polynucleotide strands wound around each other with the sugar-phosphate backbones on the outside and the nitrogenous bases facing inward. The bases on opposite strands are held together by hydrogen bonds (A-T and G-C), forming the 'rungs' of the twisted ladder.

quiz_questions.question_text: In DNA, adenine (A) always pairs with which nitrogenous base?

options.text: Uracil (U) — uracil is found in RNA, not DNA
Cytosine (C) — cytosine pairs with guanine, not adenine
Thymine (T) — adenine and thymine form two hydrogen bonds
Guanine (G) — guanine pairs with cytosine, not adenine

quiz_questions.explanation: Chargaff's rules state that in DNA, adenine (A) always pairs with thymine (T) via two hydrogen bonds, and guanine (G) always pairs with cytosine (C) via three hydrogen bonds. This complementary base pairing is essential for accurate DNA replication and transcription. Uracil replaces thymine in RNA, so A pairs with U only during transcription and translation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of chemical bond holds the two complementary strands of the DNA double helix together?

options.text: Ionic bonds — these involve transfer of electrons, not found between DNA strands
Covalent bonds — these hold nucleotides within each strand, not between strands
Hydrogen bonds — weak bonds between complementary base pairs (A-T and G-C)
Peptide bonds — these link amino acids in proteins, not nucleotides in DNA

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds hold the two DNA strands together between complementary bases: two hydrogen bonds between A-T and three between G-C. These bonds are individually weak, but collectively (billions of them in a human chromosome) they provide stability while still allowing the strands to be separated for replication and transcription. Covalent phosphodiester bonds hold nucleotides together WITHIN each strand.

quiz_questions.question_text: During DNA replication, which enzyme unwinds the double helix by breaking hydrogen bonds between base pairs?

options.text: Primase — it synthesizes short RNA primers
DNA ligase — it joins DNA fragments together
DNA polymerase — it adds new nucleotides but does not unwind DNA
Helicase — it separates the two DNA strands at the replication fork

quiz_questions.explanation: Helicase is the enzyme that 'unzips' the DNA double helix at the replication fork by using ATP energy to break the hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs. This creates two single-stranded templates that DNA polymerase can then use to synthesize new complementary strands. Without helicase, the double helix cannot be opened and replication cannot proceed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which enzyme is responsible for adding new nucleotides to a growing DNA strand during replication?

options.text: DNA polymerase — it reads the template strand and adds complementary nucleotides in the 5′ to 3′ direction
RNA polymerase — this enzyme synthesizes RNA during transcription, not DNA during replication
Ligase — this enzyme joins DNA fragments but does not add individual nucleotides
Helicase — this enzyme unwinds DNA but does not add nucleotides

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA polymerase is the main enzyme of replication. It reads the template strand in the 3′→5′ direction and synthesizes the new complementary strand in the 5′→3′ direction, adding nucleotides one at a time following base pairing rules (A with T, G with C). DNA polymerase also has proofreading ability, removing incorrectly paired nucleotides to maintain an error rate of roughly 1 in a billion bases.

quiz_questions.question_text: DNA replication is described as semiconservative. What does this mean?

options.text: Old and new DNA segments are randomly scattered throughout both daughter molecules
Only half of the genome is copied during each round of cell division
Each new DNA molecule contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesized strand
The original DNA molecule is completely conserved and an entirely new copy is made alongside it

quiz_questions.explanation: Semiconservative replication means each daughter DNA molecule retains ('conserves') one original parental strand while the other strand is newly synthesized. This was confirmed by the Meselson-Stahl experiment (1958), which used heavy (15N) and light (14N) nitrogen isotopes to track old versus new DNA strands. After one round of replication, all DNA had intermediate density, proving each molecule was half old, half new.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which enzyme synthesizes mRNA from a DNA template during transcription?

options.text: Ribosome — this reads mRNA during translation, not during transcription
Helicase — this enzyme unwinds DNA but does not synthesize RNA
DNA polymerase — this enzyme copies DNA during replication, not transcription
RNA polymerase — it reads the DNA template strand and builds a complementary mRNA strand

quiz_questions.explanation: RNA polymerase is the enzyme responsible for transcription. It binds to the promoter region of a gene, unwinds a small section of DNA, reads the template strand 3′→5′, and synthesizes a complementary mRNA strand 5′→3′. Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase does NOT require a primer to begin synthesis. In eukaryotes, RNA polymerase II is specifically responsible for transcribing protein-coding genes into mRNA.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does translation (protein synthesis) take place in a eukaryotic cell?

options.text: In the nucleus — this is where transcription occurs, not translation
In the mitochondria — some mitochondrial proteins are made here, but most translation occurs in the cytoplasm
In the Golgi apparatus — this organelle modifies and packages proteins after they are made
At ribosomes in the cytoplasm — mRNA is decoded into a polypeptide chain here

quiz_questions.explanation: Translation occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells. After mRNA is transcribed and processed in the nucleus, it is exported through nuclear pores into the cytoplasm where ribosomes (composed of rRNA and proteins) read the mRNA codons and assemble amino acids into a polypeptide chain. Ribosomes can be free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the universal start codon that signals the beginning of translation?

options.text: UAA — this is a stop codon, not a start codon
UAG — this is a stop codon, not a start codon
UGA — this is a stop codon, not a start codon
AUG — it codes for methionine and is the first codon read by the ribosome

quiz_questions.explanation: AUG is the universal start codon. It serves a dual purpose: it signals the ribosome to begin translation AND it codes for the amino acid methionine. Every newly synthesized protein begins with methionine (though it may be removed later by post-translational processing). The three stop codons — UAA, UAG, and UGA — signal termination of translation and do not code for any amino acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which molecule carries amino acids to the ribosome during translation?

options.text: mRNA (messenger RNA) — it carries the genetic code but does not transport amino acids
rRNA (ribosomal RNA) — it is a structural component of ribosomes, not an amino acid carrier
DNA — it stores genetic information in the nucleus and does not participate directly in translation
tRNA (transfer RNA) — it has an anticodon that matches the mRNA codon and carries the corresponding amino acid

quiz_questions.explanation: Transfer RNA (tRNA) is the adaptor molecule of translation. Each tRNA has two critical regions: an anticodon (three nucleotides complementary to an mRNA codon) and an amino acid attachment site at its 3′ end. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase enzymes charge each tRNA with the correct amino acid. During translation, tRNA anticodons base-pair with mRNA codons in the ribosome, delivering amino acids in the order specified by the mRNA.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines DNA replication?

options.text: The process of copying a gene's DNA sequence into a messenger RNA molecule
The process by which a cell copies its entire DNA molecule before cell division, producing two identical double helices
The process of assembling amino acids into a protein using information from mRNA
The breakdown and recycling of old DNA molecules during programmed cell death

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA replication is the biological process by which a cell duplicates its entire DNA, producing two identical copies from one original molecule. This occurs during the S phase of the cell cycle, before mitosis or meiosis, ensuring each daughter cell receives a complete genome. Key enzymes include helicase (unwinds), primase (lays RNA primers), DNA polymerase (adds nucleotides), and ligase (seals fragments).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines transcription in molecular biology?

options.text: The synthesis of an mRNA molecule from a DNA template by RNA polymerase, occurring in the nucleus of eukaryotes
The duplication of the entire DNA molecule before cell division
The assembly of a polypeptide chain from mRNA instructions at the ribosome
The folding of a polypeptide chain into its three-dimensional functional shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Transcription is the first step of gene expression in which RNA polymerase reads a DNA template strand (3′→5′) and synthesizes a complementary mRNA strand (5′→3′). In eukaryotes this occurs in the nucleus. The resulting pre-mRNA undergoes processing (5′ cap, poly-A tail, intron splicing) to produce mature mRNA, which is then exported to the cytoplasm for translation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines translation in molecular biology?

options.text: The process by which ribosomes decode mRNA codons and assemble a specific sequence of amino acids into a polypeptide
The copying of DNA into a complementary mRNA strand by RNA polymerase
The transport of mRNA from the nucleus to the cytoplasm through nuclear pores
The duplication of the entire genome before cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: Translation is the second step of gene expression where the ribosome reads mRNA in sets of three nucleotides (codons), each specifying an amino acid. Transfer RNA (tRNA) molecules deliver the correct amino acids, which are joined by peptide bonds into a growing polypeptide chain. Translation begins at the start codon AUG and continues until a stop codon (UAA, UAG, or UGA) is reached.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a codon?

options.text: A sequence of three consecutive nucleotides on mRNA that specifies a particular amino acid or a stop signal
A single nucleotide in a DNA strand that encodes one amino acid
A chemical bond that links amino acids together in a polypeptide chain
A protein that assists in the folding of other proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: A codon is a triplet of three consecutive nucleotides on mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid or serves as a stop signal. With four possible RNA bases (A, U, G, C) in groups of three, there are 64 possible codons: 61 specify amino acids and 3 (UAA, UAG, UGA) are stop codons. The genetic code is degenerate, meaning most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Central Dogma of molecular biology?

options.text: The idea that all living cells arise from pre-existing cells
The principle that genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to protein (DNA → RNA → Protein)
The concept that genetic mutations are always harmful to organisms
The rule that DNA must replicate before every cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: The Central Dogma, proposed by Francis Crick in 1958, states that genetic information flows in one general direction: DNA is transcribed into RNA, and RNA is translated into protein. Information does not flow backward from protein to nucleic acid. Exceptions include reverse transcriptase (RNA → DNA, found in retroviruses like HIV) and RNA replicase (RNA → RNA), but protein → nucleic acid transfer has never been observed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary function of messenger RNA (mRNA)?

options.text: It carries the genetic code copied from DNA in the nucleus to ribosomes in the cytoplasm for translation
It forms the structural and catalytic core of ribosomes
It carries amino acids to the ribosome and matches them to the correct mRNA codons
It unwinds the DNA double helix during replication

quiz_questions.explanation: Messenger RNA (mRNA) serves as the intermediary between DNA and protein. During transcription, the information in a gene's DNA is copied into mRNA. This mRNA then exits the nucleus and travels to ribosomes in the cytoplasm, where its codon sequence is read during translation to direct the assembly of a specific protein. mRNA is a temporary molecule that is eventually degraded after being translated.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary function of transfer RNA (tRNA)?

options.text: It carries specific amino acids to the ribosome and uses its anticodon to match them to the correct mRNA codon
It carries the genetic code from DNA to the ribosome for translation
It forms the structural framework and catalytic center of the ribosome
It synthesizes new DNA strands by adding nucleotides during replication

quiz_questions.explanation: Transfer RNA (tRNA) is the adaptor molecule that bridges the nucleotide language of mRNA and the amino acid language of proteins. Each tRNA molecule has an anticodon (complementary to an mRNA codon) at one end and carries the corresponding amino acid at its other end. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases are enzymes that 'charge' each tRNA with the correct amino acid, ensuring translation accuracy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary function of ribosomal RNA (rRNA)?

options.text: It forms the structural and catalytic core of the ribosome, the molecular machine where translation occurs
It transports amino acids to the ribosome during translation
It copies the DNA molecule during replication
It carries the genetic message from DNA to the ribosome

quiz_questions.explanation: Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is the most abundant type of RNA in cells and makes up about 60% of the ribosome's mass. rRNA provides both structural scaffolding and catalytic activity — it is the rRNA (not ribosomal proteins) that catalyzes the formation of peptide bonds between amino acids, making the ribosome a ribozyme. Ribosomes have two subunits (large and small) that come together around mRNA during translation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an anticodon?

options.text: An enzyme that degrades codons after translation is complete
A signal on mRNA that tells the ribosome to stop translating
A three-nucleotide sequence on mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid
A three-nucleotide sequence on tRNA that is complementary and antiparallel to an mRNA codon

quiz_questions.explanation: An anticodon is a set of three nucleotides on the bottom loop of a tRNA molecule that is complementary to a codon on the mRNA. During translation, the anticodon base-pairs with the mRNA codon in an antiparallel fashion (e.g., mRNA codon 5′-GCA-3′ pairs with tRNA anticodon 3′-CGU-5′). This ensures the correct amino acid is added to the growing polypeptide chain.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of DNA polymerase during DNA replication?

options.text: It joins Okazaki fragments together on the lagging strand
It synthesizes new DNA strands by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand in the 5′→3′ direction
It unwinds the DNA double helix at the replication fork
It synthesizes mRNA from a DNA template during transcription

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA polymerase is the central enzyme of replication. It reads the template strand in the 3′→5′ direction and adds complementary deoxyribonucleotides to the new strand in the 5′→3′ direction. DNA polymerase also has 3′→5′ exonuclease (proofreading) activity, allowing it to remove and correct mismatched nucleotides. On the leading strand it works continuously; on the lagging strand it synthesizes short Okazaki fragments.

quiz_questions.question_text: A DNA template strand reads 3′-TACGGATTC-5′. During transcription, RNA polymerase uses this strand to build an mRNA molecule. What is the resulting mRNA sequence?

options.text: 5′-AUGCCUAAG-3′ — each DNA base is matched with its RNA complement (T→A, A→U, C→G, G→C)
3′-AUGCCUAAG-5′ — the bases are correct but the direction is reversed; mRNA is built 5′→3′
5′-ATGCCTAAG-3′ — this uses thymine instead of uracil, which would be DNA, not mRNA
5′-TACGGATTC-3′ — this is simply a copy of the template strand, not its complement

quiz_questions.explanation: To determine the mRNA sequence, apply complementary base pairing to the DNA template strand, remembering that RNA uses uracil (U) instead of thymine (T). Template 3′-TACGGATTC-5′ yields mRNA 5′-AUGCCUAAG-3′: T→A, A→U, C→G, G→C, G→C, A→U, T→A, T→A, C→G. The mRNA strand is always written 5′→3′ because that is the direction of synthesis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mutation changes the mRNA codon GAG (which codes for glutamic acid) to GUG (which codes for valine). The resulting protein has a different amino acid at that position, altering its function. What type of mutation is this?

options.text: Frameshift mutation — this would require an insertion or deletion shifting the reading frame
Nonsense mutation — this would create a premature stop codon, not substitute an amino acid
Silent mutation — the amino acid would remain the same despite the codon change
Missense mutation — a single nucleotide change results in a different amino acid being incorporated

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a classic missense mutation — a single nucleotide substitution (A→U in the second position) changes the codon from GAG to GUG, replacing glutamic acid with valine. This exact mutation causes sickle cell disease in the beta-globin gene. Missense mutations alter protein function when the substituted amino acid has different chemical properties (e.g., hydrophilic glutamic acid vs. hydrophobic valine).

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher discovers that cells treated with a chemical inhibitor of helicase cannot complete DNA replication. What is the most direct reason for this failure?

options.text: Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand cannot be joined together
mRNA molecules cannot be translated into proteins at the ribosome
RNA primers cannot be synthesized on the template strands
The DNA double helix cannot be unwound, so the template strands are not exposed for copying

quiz_questions.explanation: Helicase is the first enzyme to act at the replication fork — it breaks hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs to separate the two strands of the double helix. If helicase is inhibited, the DNA remains wound up and the single-stranded templates needed by DNA polymerase are never exposed. Joining Okazaki fragments is ligase's job, primer synthesis is primase's job, and translation is unrelated to replication.

quiz_questions.question_text: An mRNA strand contains the following codons: AUG-GGC-UAC-UGA. How many amino acids will be incorporated into the polypeptide chain before translation stops?

options.text: 2 amino acids — only AUG and GGC are translated before the ribosome stops
4 amino acids — all four codons including UGA code for amino acids
3 amino acids (Met-Gly-Tyr) — AUG starts translation, GGC and UAC each add an amino acid, and UGA is a stop codon
1 amino acid — only the start codon AUG is translated

quiz_questions.explanation: Reading the mRNA codons: AUG = methionine (start), GGC = glycine, UAC = tyrosine, UGA = stop. The ribosome translates three codons into amino acids (Met-Gly-Tyr) before reaching the stop codon UGA, which signals release factor proteins to terminate translation. The stop codon does NOT code for an amino acid — it terminates the polypeptide chain. The resulting tripeptide is methionine-glycine-tyrosine.

quiz_questions.question_text: During translation, the ribosome is positioned at the mRNA codon 5′-GCA-3′. A tRNA molecule must deliver the amino acid alanine. What is the anticodon sequence on this tRNA?

options.text: 3′-CGU-5′ — the anticodon is complementary and antiparallel to the mRNA codon
5′-CGU-3′ — these are the correct complementary bases but in the wrong orientation
5′-GCA-3′ — this is just a copy of the mRNA codon in the same direction
3′-GCA-5′ — this is identical to the codon, not complementary to it

quiz_questions.explanation: The tRNA anticodon must be complementary AND antiparallel to the mRNA codon. For mRNA codon 5′-GCA-3′: G pairs with C, C pairs with G, A pairs with U. The anticodon reads 3′-CGU-5′. Directionality is critical — mRNA and tRNA strands run antiparallel to each other during codon-anticodon base pairing, just as the two strands of DNA are antiparallel.

quiz_questions.question_text: A point mutation changes the mRNA codon CUU (leucine) to CUC. Consulting a codon table reveals that CUC also codes for leucine. What type of mutation is this, and what is its effect on the protein?

options.text: Silent (synonymous) mutation — the nucleotide changes but the amino acid remains leucine, so the protein is unaffected
Frameshift mutation — the reading frame shifts, changing all downstream amino acids
Missense mutation — a different amino acid is substituted, altering the protein
Nonsense mutation — a premature stop codon is created, truncating the protein

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a silent (synonymous) mutation — the codon changes from CUU to CUC, but both code for leucine. The protein's amino acid sequence is completely unaffected. Silent mutations are possible because the genetic code is degenerate (redundant), meaning most amino acids are encoded by multiple codons. Changes in the third (wobble) position of a codon often result in silent mutations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mutation changes the mRNA codon UAC (tyrosine) to the stop codon UAA. What is the most likely consequence for the protein being synthesized?

options.text: The protein will be completely unaffected because the genetic code is redundant
The protein will be truncated (shorter than normal) because translation terminates prematurely at the new stop codon
The protein will have only one amino acid substitution but otherwise function normally
The protein will be longer than normal because the ribosome reads past the new codon

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a nonsense mutation — a codon that normally specifies an amino acid (UAC = tyrosine) is changed to a premature stop codon (UAA). When the ribosome reaches this new stop codon, translation terminates early, producing a truncated protein that is usually nonfunctional. Nonsense mutations are typically more harmful than missense mutations because they can eliminate large portions of the protein's amino acid sequence.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cell is treated with a drug that specifically inhibits RNA polymerase. Which cellular process is directly blocked, and what downstream effect does this have?

options.text: Transcription is blocked — without new mRNA, the cell eventually cannot produce new proteins via translation
Translation is directly blocked — ribosomes cannot assemble amino acids into proteins
DNA replication is blocked — the cell cannot copy its genome for cell division
DNA repair is blocked — damaged nucleotides cannot be replaced

quiz_questions.explanation: RNA polymerase is the enzyme that performs transcription (DNA → mRNA). If it is inhibited, no new mRNA can be synthesized. Since mRNA is the template for protein synthesis, the downstream effect is that translation will also eventually halt as existing mRNA molecules are degraded and not replaced. DNA replication uses DNA polymerase, not RNA polymerase, and is not directly affected by this drug.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studying DNA replication discovers that a cell line has a mutation causing DNA ligase to be nonfunctional. Which of the following problems would be observed during replication?

options.text: The leading strand will not be synthesized continuously
RNA primers will not be synthesized on either strand
Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand will not be joined, leaving gaps in the sugar-phosphate backbone
The DNA double helix will not be unwound at the replication fork

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA ligase seals the phosphodiester bonds between Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand after DNA polymerase I has replaced the RNA primers with DNA. Without functional ligase, these fragments remain disconnected, leaving nicks (breaks) in the sugar-phosphate backbone of the new lagging strand. The leading strand is synthesized continuously and requires only one primer, so it is much less affected by ligase deficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A two-nucleotide insertion occurs near the beginning of the coding region of an mRNA. How does this affect every codon downstream of the insertion?

options.text: It causes the ribosome to skip two codons and resume normal reading downstream
It causes a frameshift — the reading frame shifts so that every codon after the insertion is misread, likely producing a nonfunctional protein
It changes only the single codon at the insertion site, leaving downstream codons unaffected
It has no effect because the genetic code's redundancy compensates for the extra nucleotides

quiz_questions.explanation: A two-nucleotide insertion shifts the reading frame — since codons are read in non-overlapping triplets, adding two nucleotides causes every subsequent codon to be read incorrectly. This frameshift mutation typically introduces wrong amino acids and often creates a premature stop codon, producing a truncated, nonfunctional protein. Only insertions or deletions in multiples of three preserve the reading frame (though they still add or remove amino acids).
